stories.title: Retail, Multifamily CMBS Delinquencies Rise in June as Overall Rate Moves Lower
stories.excerpt: The Trepp CMBS Delinquency Rate decreased by 20 basis points to 7.35% in June 2026, led by a large lodging cure. However, three of the five major property types saw increases in their delinquency rates during the month, while two moved lower. The five largest newly delinquent loans accounted for $998.9 million of the $2.64 billion in newly delinquent loans. They included a super-regional mall in Southern California, a regional mall in New Hampshire, an office complex in New York, a mixed-use tower in Minneapolis and a Manhattan multifamily property. Retail posted the largest increase, rising 30 bps to 6.91%, and multifamily increased 28 bps to 7.23%, reversing last month’s improvement as several large multifamily assets turned delinquent. Office edged up four bps to 11.57%. Lodging posted the largest decrease, declining 79 basis points to 5.22%, and industrial declined 11 basis points to 1.20%. So-called CMBS 2.0 loans posted similar results in June for all five property types. The post Retail, Multifamily CMBS Delinquencies Rise in June as Overall Rate Moves Lower appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Multifamily CMBS delinquencies rose 28 bps to 7.23% in June 2026; several large assets turned delinquent.
Manhattan multifamily property among five largest newly delinquent loans totaling $2.64B.
Retail delinquencies up 30 bps to 6.91%; office up 4 bps to 11.57%.
stories.summary: Multifamily CMBS delinquencies jumped 28 basis points to 7.23% in June 2026, reversing prior-month gains as several major assets entered default. The sector's deterioration was part of a broader uneven picture: retail spiked 30 bps to 6.91%, while office edged up 4 bps to 11.57%. Lodging was the only bright spot, declining 79 bps to 5.22%.
stories.impact: Rising multifamily delinquency rates signal stressed assets entering the market, particularly in high-cost urban markets like Manhattan. This distress cycle typically precedes significant repricing and dispositions, creating acquisition windows for well-capitalized developers—but also flagging deteriorating underwriting fundamentals in prior vintage cohorts and tightening lender appetite for new multifamily originations across the board.

stories.title: Return to Lender: Week of July 2, 2026
stories.excerpt: A retail unit at The Royale, a residential condominium building in Manhattan’s Lenox Hill neighborhood, has returned to its lender. The New York Business Journal reported that MetLife acquired the garage and commercial unit at 188 E. 64th St. for $44.1 million, according to a deed in lieu of foreclosure filed June 29 in New York City property records. The insurance giant is an active commercial lender through its subsidiary, MetLife Investment Management. The company sold the InterContinental New York Times Square Hotel late last year and took over a foreclosed office building in Manhattan’s Garment District in July 2024. A pair of Old Town Alexandria office buildings on the waterfront are scheduled for upcoming receivership auctions, with one pitched as a potential residential conversion, the Washington Business Journal reported. Real Insight Marketplace and Transwestern’s Mid-Atlantic Capital Markets Group are putting the leasehold interest on 11 and 99 Canal Center Plaza, collectively built in 1987, up for auction. They’re part of the four-building Canal Center office complex. The other two buildings in the complex, which has an assessed value of $126.8 million, are not part of the auctions, which is scheduled for August 3-5. The Hillsboro Beach Resort, along the ocean in the wealthy town of Hillsboro Beach, FL, is headed to auction after its owner lost a $40-million foreclosure judgment, the South Florida Business Journal reported. Broward County Circuit Court Judge Martin Bidwill awarded the judgment in favor of lender Emerald Creek Capital 3 LLC and against borrowers BNH IV HM TRI LLC and 1159 Hillsboro Mile LLC regarding a mortgage with $26 million in principal outstanding, plus interest and fees. The 81-unit hotel, on 1.93 acres at 1159 Hillsboro Mile, is scheduled for auction July 16. AuctionAdvisors has been retained to conduct the receivership auction of four income-producing investment properties located in Orange and Newark, NJ. The pr
bullets: MetLife acquired retail/garage at 188 E. 64th St., Manhattan, via deed in lieu for $44.1M.
Two Old Town Alexandria office buildings (11, 99 Canal Center Plaza) headed to receivership auction Aug 3-5; one marketed for residential conversion.
Hillsboro Beach Resort (81 units) going to auction after $40M foreclosure judgment; $26M principal outstanding.
stories.summary: MetLife Investment Management took a retail/garage unit at Lenox Hill Manhattan via deed-in-lieu foreclosure ($44.1M), while two Alexandria office buildings are scheduled for August receivership auction with one positioned for residential conversion. The Hillsboro Beach Resort (81-unit hotel on 1.93 acres) will also hit auction following a $40M foreclosure judgment with $26M in outstanding principal.
stories.impact: The emergence of office-to-residential conversion opportunities in secondary markets (Alexandria waterfront) and hotel-to-multifamily potential signal distressed capital reallocating from obsolete asset classes. Manhattan's deed-in-lieu activity hints at mixed-use and retail-heavy properties facing refinance cliffs; developers should monitor these auctions for shovel-ready adaptive-reuse sites with below-market acquisition costs.

stories.title: Connect Midwest Multifamily Trends 2026: View from the Top
stories.excerpt: The Midwest multifamily markets have been among the leaders in annual rent growth for the past few years, attracting investors and developers. At the recent Connect Midwest Multifamily Trends 2026 event in Chicago, hundreds of industry professionals gathered to hear insights into the opportunities and challenges facing the sector across the region. In a panel discussion moderated by Aaron May, Partner at Gould & Ratner LLP, multifamily leaders discussed the most active sales and development markets, whether urban or suburban is better poised for growth, and which projects are in demand and closing successfully. We strongly feel some of the best opportunities are here in Chicago, especially suburban Chicago,” said Trevor Ryan, President of Marquette Companies. “Elsewhere in the Midwest, some of our capital partners continually bring up markets like Columbus, Indianapolis, Kansas City, Madison, and Milwaukee.” “The thing that’s made development work in the city now is the new tax abatement,” explained Darren Sloniger, Founder and Owner of Parq Development. “The tax abatement has really provided new life for development in Chicago. There’s a big pipeline out there of deals that want to get financed, and it’s going to be the best located properties with the best strategies that are going to get done.” Speaking to successful deals and projects coming to fruition, Peter Gudonis, Head of Development at CityPads, noted, “We just sold the first development of our current fund. Everyone’s happy, it established our proof of concept, and we’re very excited for the group.” In the suburban markets and the secondary and tertiary markets, I think there’s going to be such a huge wave of distress that’s going to hit, that it’s going to present a huge opportunity in those areas,” added Reid Bennett, Senior Vice President for SVN – Chicago Commercial. ” But at the same
bullets: Midwest multifamily leaders cite Chicago, suburban Chicago, Columbus, Indianapolis, Kansas City, Madison, Milwaukee as top opportunities.
Chicago new tax abatement policy enabling development pipeline; best-located, best-strategy deals getting financed.
CityPads sold first development in current fund; proof of concept achieved, pipeline advancing.
stories.summary: Midwest multifamily professionals at Connect Midwest 2026 conference identified Chicago (especially suburban), Columbus, Indianapolis, Kansas City, Madison, and Milwaukee as prime markets. Chicago's new municipal tax abatement has catalyzed a significant development pipeline, with speakers noting that financing now favors best-located properties with sound underwriting. CityPads announced successful exit on inaugural fund deal, validating current-market execution.
stories.impact: Chicago's tax abatement renewal is a major demand signal for development capital—it directly improves project returns and unlocks previously marginal deals. The convergence of multifamily leadership on secondary Midwest metros (Columbus, Indianapolis) suggests those markets are absorbing capital flight from oversupplied Sunbelt and coastal metros; developers should prioritize underwriting in these submarkets ahead of major corporate relocations or population inflows that typically follow investor precedent.

stories.title: Slatt Capital Arranges Acquisition Financing for Home2 Suites in Arkansas
stories.excerpt: Slatt Capital arranged a $9.49 million acquisition loan for the Home2 Suites, a 90-room hospitality property located in Fort Smith, Arkansas. Built in 2016, the property was financed on a three-year term with a 25-year amortization schedule and an 18-month interest-only period. Slatt Capital’s Neil Holliday led the transaction, with Bret Piekaar serving as analyst. The financing was secured through Chambers Bank and supported the buyer’s acquisition of the well-located, branded hospitality asset. “Home2 Suites is a strong, stabilized asset in the Fort Smith market, and we were able to secure financing that reflects both the quality of the property and the strength of the sponsorship,” said Holliday. The property’s strong performance, brand affiliation, and Fort Smith location were key factors in underwriting the loan. Located at 7400 Phoenix Avenue, the property offers easy access to Fort Smith Airport and Mercy Hospital, Parrot Island Water Park, and the Fort Smith Regional Art Museum. The post Slatt Capital Arranges Acquisition Financing for Home2 Suites in Arkansas appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Slatt Capital arranged $9.49M acquisition loan for 90-room Home2 Suites in Fort Smith, Arkansas.
Three-year term, 25-year amortization, 18-month interest-only; Chambers Bank executed.
Property built 2016, stabilized, branded hospitality asset with strategic airport/hospital adjacency.
stories.summary: Slatt Capital closed a $9.49M acquisition financing for a 90-room Home2 Suites built in 2016 in Fort Smith, Arkansas. The loan featured a 3-year term, 25-year amortization, and 18-month interest-only period via Chambers Bank. Deal rationale centered on the property's stabilized cash flow, brand affiliation, and favorable location near Fort Smith Airport and Mercy Hospital.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance; hospitality financing environment in secondary markets remains active, but this deal is not a demand or supply signal for apartment development. Fort Smith is not a multifamily bellwether.

stories.title: Greater L.A. Office Leasing Shows Signs of Life — Finally
stories.excerpt: It’s still not “back,” but it’s getting more difficult to say Los Angeles’ office market is “dead.” Tenants signed for nearly 4 million square feet of office space across Greater L.A. in the second quarter, according to a new report from Savills. That’s almost 15 percent more than the previous quarter, and almost 8 percent more than the same period last year. The quarterly total is also 22 percent above the five-year quarterly average for office leases, and is approaching levels last seen in 2019. However, Savills reported the recovery remains uneven, and it is still largely fueled by renewals and relocations rather than new demand. Bank of America signed the largest deal in the quarter with a 137,873-square-foot renewal and expansion with Hines and J.P. Morgan Chase at the south tower of the prominent Century Park Plaza at 2049 Century Park East in Century City. Other notable deals included LA28’s 57,512-square-foot expansion in Downtown L.A., as well as Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals’ 52,568-square-foot renewal and 49,449-square-foot expansion with Rockpoint at 177 East Colorado Boulevard in Pasadena. Meanwhile, another 2.8 million square feet of available sublease space came off the board over the past 12 months for a nearly 30 percent annual decline. Total available sublease space also fell from 7.4 million square feet to 6.7 million square feet in the second quarter alone, marking the seventh straight quarter of declining sublease supply. L.A.’s sublease space peaked at 11 million square feet in 2024. Savills noted some of the improvement in sublease availability resulted from leases expiring and reverting to general availability, rather than tenants actively absorbing the secondhand space. As such, overall availability in L.A. stood at 26.6 percent in the second quarter, down just 130 basis points from a year ago, despite the 4 million square feet reported. Similarly, average direct asking rent was $4.19 per square foot per month in the second quarter, down 0.4 per
bullets: Greater L.A. office leasing 3.97M SF in Q2 2026, +15% QoQ, +8% YoY, +22% vs. 5-yr avg.
Bank of America 137.9K SF renewal/expansion; LA28 57.5K SF expansion; recovery driven by renewals/relocations.
Leasing velocity approaching 2019 levels but demand remains muted for new office.
stories.summary: Greater Los Angeles office leasing reached 3.97 million square feet in Q2 2026, up 15% sequentially and 8% year-over-year, approaching 2019 run rates. Large tenants like Bank of America, LA28, and Arrowhead Pharma drove volume via renewals and expansions. However, Savills noted the recovery remains skewed toward renewals and relocations rather than net new demand, with few requirements driving new office absorption.
stories.impact: L.A. office stabilization suggests reduced conversion urgency for office-to-multifamily adaptive reuse projects; strong renewal activity keeps landlords solvent and less willing to entertain repurposing. However, this recovery is fragile and tied to corporate consolidation, not growth—developers should monitor if this momentum sustains through 2027 or if office slips again, triggering a fresh wave of conversion opportunities.

stories.title: Charney Acquires 143 Roebling in Williamsburg
stories.excerpt: Charney Companies has completed the $20-million purchase of 143 Roebling St., a 100,000-square-foot building in Williamsburg, Brooklyn. Financing was provided by BH3 Fund Advisors in a structured transaction. Charney plans to fortify and restore the property. “We are very excited to be taking over ownership for this building and know local residents will be as well,” said Justin Pelsinger, COO at Charney Companies. “We are looking forward to restoring and modernizing this neighborhood gem to better serve Brooklynites for generations to come.” The building was originally built in 1907 and has undergone several renovations throughout the years. It has provided both residential and retail spaces, including the Roebling Tea Room, but has been vacant for the past few years. JLL represented the seller, Los Angeles-based institutional lender Calmwater Capital, and negotiated directly with Charney Companies. The JLL Capital Markets Investment Sales and Advisory team representing the seller was led by Ethan Stanton, Brendan Maddigan and Michael Mazzara. The post Charney Acquires 143 Roebling in Williamsburg appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Charney Companies acquired 143 Roebling St., Williamsburg, Brooklyn for $20M; 100K SF, built 1907.
Property vacant for years; includes residential and retail (Roebling Tea Room); BH3 Fund structured financing.
Plan: fortify, restore, and reactivate mixed-use asset.
stories.summary: Charney Companies completed a $20M acquisition of 143 Roebling Street in Williamsburg, Brooklyn—a 100,000-square-foot, 1907-built mixed-use property that has been vacant for several years. BH3 Fund Advisors provided structured financing. Charney plans restoration and modernization to reactivate residential and retail components, including the shuttered Roebling Tea Room.
stories.impact: Classic Williamsburg adaptive-reuse play; acquisition at $200/SF indicates stabilized valuation in a highly desirable submarket. This signals continued investor confidence in Brooklyn's core neighborhoods and availability of patient capital for repositioning. Developers should monitor Charney's execution timeline—successful rents and lease velocity will confirm market appetite for renovated product in North Brooklyn.

stories.title: Zar Property NY Buys 118 West 22nd Street for $28M
stories.excerpt: A GFP Real Estate-managed mixed-use building in Manhattan’s Flatiron District has changed hands. GFP manages the 12-story property at 118 West 22nd Street for owner Adlie Associates, which sold the building to Zar Property NY for $28 million, with a $19.6 million financing package from Filler Capital, according to property records made public Wednesday. Adlie appears to have acquired the property in 1986 for an unknown amount, the earliest deed filings indicate. Zar and GFP, which does not own a stake in the building, declined to comment. Isaac Filler of Filler Capital took to LinkedIn to comment on the deal, describing the financing deal as a “meaningful” milestone in his relationship with Zar Property’s Simon Zar, marking an 18-year business relationship that started with lunch at a real estate conference in 2008. “By pairing a best-in-class sponsor with the right lending partner, we were able to secure exceptional financing terms from J.P. Morgan,” Filler said in a statement. Notable tenants in the building include Walter B. Melvin Architects and Kleinfeld Bridal Boutique. NY Cake operates out of the ground-floor retail space, where it signed a 10,500-square-foot lease in April 2018, Commercial Observer reported at the time. The 100,000-square-foot building on 22nd Street between Seventh Avenue and Avenue of the Americas was built in 1911 with designs by architect Frederick Zobel, who designed a number of structures now protected by the New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission, such as 35 West 39th Street. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Zar Property NY purchased 118 W. 22nd St., Flatiron, Manhattan for $28M with $19.6M financing from Filler Capital.
12-story mixed-use building; JP Morgan loan structured; 18-year lender relationship highlight.
Tenants include Walter B. Melvin Architects, Kleinfeld Bridal, NY Cake; acquired by prior owner in 1986.
stories.summary: Zar Property NY acquired 118 West 22nd Street in Flatiron, Manhattan for $28M, with Filler Capital providing $19.6M in financing structured through JP Morgan. The 12-story mixed-use property houses Kleinfeld Bridal and other retail/office tenants. The deal highlighted an 18-year lender-sponsor relationship and was characterized as achieving strong financing terms.
stories.impact: Manhattan mixed-use acquisitions at $2.3M/floor in Flatiron suggest stable pricing for stabilized, multi-tenant buildings with diverse tenant bases. Lender willingness to execute at scale signals consistent capital availability for core NYC product, though pricing discipline remains tight. Developers should note Flatiron's durability as a mixed-use submarket, but conversion upside remains limited given existing strong rents and well-leased status.

stories.title: Klyde Warren Park expansion set to bring $4B in real estate value to Dallas
stories.excerpt: Klyde Warren Park, which marks the transition from Downtown Dallas to Uptown, is set to expand by nearly two acres. The park’s chairman, Jody Grant, said that the 1.7 acre expansion of the park could create around $3.5 billion in new real estate value in the area via investment and tourism, according to the Dallas Business Journal. Grant also claims that the park has added $5.9 billion in real estate value for the Arts District and Uptown. The added value has filled the pockets of the city, Dallas County, and DISD to the tune of $957 million in incremental tax […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Klyde Warren Park 1.7-acre westward expansion (Jacobs Lawn) expected to generate $3.5–4B in new real estate value.
Park has already created $5.9B cumulative value for Arts District/Uptown; generated $957M in incremental tax revenue.
24K SF event/reception space plus retail; ice rink in winter; Archer & Wester/Gensler/HKS designers.
stories.summary: Klyde Warren Park's planned 1.7-acre westward expansion into Jacobs Lawn is projected to generate $3.5–4B in new real estate value via incremental investment and tourism. The original park has created $5.9B in cumulative value and $957M in tax revenue for Dallas, Arts District, and Uptown. The expansion will include 37K SF of green space and a 24K SF event/reception venue with ground-floor retail.
stories.impact: Klyde Warren Park's proven track record of catalyzing real estate value ($5.9B over time) signals strong tailwinds for multifamily development in adjacent Uptown—the expansion will likely accelerate asset sales, entitlements, and construction starts in surrounding blocks. However, Downtown Dallas's structural headwinds (office exodus, sports team departures, Neiman Marcus loss) mean the Uptown leverage may mask or delay broader CBD softness; developers should prioritize Uptown-adjacent sites while monitoring Downtown conversion feasibility separately.

stories.title: Mixed-use mania: Mapping mall redevelopments planned in South Florida
stories.excerpt: Many South Florida shopping malls are like chrysalises, struggling to shed their retail-only past and emerge as vibrant mixed-use districts. More than a half-dozen shopping centers across Miami-Dade, Broward and Palm Beach counties are slated for redevelopments that would add thousands of apartments alongside retail, restaurants, offices, hotels and entertainment. The projects reflect the region’s demand for housing and owners’ efforts to revive malls that have lost department stores, grappled with declining foot traffic or struggled to fill vacant space.  But that push for growth hasn’t been without challenges, as several redevelopment plans have encountered political opposition, legal battles or […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: South Florida: 6+ mall redevelopments adding thousands of apartments; Miami Intl Mall 896-unit Greystar project (Doral); Kohl's converting to 110K SF entertainment.
Easton Group acquired Sears/JCPenney parcels at Miami Intl for $29.3M (2024); Doral City Council advanced first reading June 2026.
Projects face political opposition, legal delays; demand for housing and owner intent to revive struggling retail hubs.
stories.summary: More than a half-dozen South Florida shopping centers (Miami-Dade, Broward, Palm Beach) are planning mixed-use redevelopments with thousands of proposed apartments. Simon Property Group's Miami International Mall in Doral is advancing a Greystar 896-unit apartment project on former Sears/JCPenney sites acquired by Easton Group for $29.3M; Doral City Council advanced the application on first reading in June. Redevelopments face obstacles including political opposition and legal challenges.
stories.impact: South Florida mall redevelopments represent a massive pipeline of entitled or nearly-entitled multifamily sites in secondary/tertiary submarkets (Doral, Coral Gables, Broward). Greystar's lead role signals institutional capital is already pre-leasing risk; however, political headwinds and zoning battles suggest execution risk. Developers should prioritize sites with clear entitlements and municipal support, while monitoring which projects get stalled—those may offer acquisition opportunities at distressed pricing if sponsors lose momentum or capital.

stories.title: Obra Supplies $31M Refi on Arizona Northrop Grumman-Leased Office Property
stories.excerpt: Silver Creek Development has secured $31.4 million of bridge debt to refinance a single-tenant office complex in suburban Phoenix, Commercial Observer has learned Obra Real Estate provided the loan for the developer’s 2021-built office property at 1715 West Elliot Road in Gilbert, Ariz. The complex is fully leased to Northrop Grumman, a global aerospace and defense technology company. “This transaction reflects our continued focus on high-quality assets supported by institutional credit and experienced sponsorship,” Michael Bachenheimer, managing director at Obra Real Estate, said in a statement. “Our ability to move quickly and deliver certainty of close is a key differentiator, particularly for sponsors seeking flexible capital in today’s market.” Silver Creek acquired the 120,294-square-foot office building for $43.1 million from SunCap Property Group in early 2021, AZ Big Media previously reported. Arrow Real Estate Advisors arranged the transaction with a team consisting Morris Betesh, Eliott Zeitoune, Alex Bailkin and Andrew Rosenberg. Located 20 miles southeast of Downtown Phoenix within the 63-acre Gilbert Spectrum Business Park, the Northrop Grumman-leased property is strongly positioned from having a long-term tenant in place in one of the nation’s strongest-performing markets for corporate investment and employment growth, according to Arrow “This transaction underscores the continued demand for high-quality assets backed by institutional-caliber tenancy and experienced sponsorship,” Betesh, managing partner at Arrow Real Estate Advisors, said in a statement. “Northrop Grumman’s investment-grade profile, combined with the sponsor’s track record and active presence in the market, made this an exceptionally compelling finance opportunity that closed in under 30 days.” Silver Creek Development did not immediately return a request for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Obra Real Estate provided $31.4M bridge financing for Silver Creek's 120K SF office building in Gilbert, Arizona.
Northrop Grumman single-tenant lease (100% occupied); built 2021; acquired by Silver Creek for $43.1M in 2021 from SunCap.
Strong institutional credit and sponsorship; lender touts speed and certainty of close.
stories.summary: Obra Real Estate arranged $31.4M in bridge refinancing for Silver Creek Development's fully leased 120,294-square-foot office building in Gilbert, Arizona at 1715 West Elliot Road. The property is leased entirely to Northrop Grumman, an aerospace/defense anchor tenant. Silver Creek acquired the 2021-built asset from SunCap in early 2021 for $43.1M; the refinance structure emphasizes flexibility and sponsor-lender speed.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance; this is a stabilized single-tenant office deal. However, Phoenix's aerospace/defense industrial base (Northrop Grumman, Raytheon, etc.) drives strong office and industrial demand—developers should monitor whether such corporate anchors expand or relocate, which could trigger demand for worker housing in Gilbert and surrounding submarkets.

stories.title: Manhattan’s Office Leasing Reaches Velocity Not Seen Since 2002: Report
stories.excerpt: Manhattan’s office leasing in the first half of 2026 was one for the books. The past two quarters counted just under 23 million square feet of office leases, according to Colliers’ second-quarter office report, in what the brokerage called the strongest first-half period of leasing velocity since 2002. “You really saw recovery show itself in multiple ways,” Franklin Wallach, Colliers’ executive managing director for research and business, told Commercial Observer, “On the demand side, the supply side, the pricing.” The results are music to New York City office landlords’ ears. Demand outpaced tightening supply, law firms and tech gobbled up space, and average asking rents sharply increased. Leasing volume during the second quarter totaled 11.02 million square feet, with a positive net absorption of 3.51 million square feet. That leasing activity was led by law firm Simpson Thacher & Bartlett’s 916,000-square-foot lease at Extell Development’s office development at 570 Fifth Avenue in May. Manhattan’s law firms, with their insatiable appetite for office, occupied five of the top 10 largest lease deals in the quarter, according to Wallach. Those heavyweights included Cleary Gottlieb Steen & Hamilton’s 475,000-square-foot deal at One Liberty Plaza and Alston & Bird’s 169,664-square-foot lease at 51 West 52nd Street. Average office asking rents increased to $78.03 per square foot, up 5.7 percent annually. According to Colliers, that’s the sharpest midyear annual growth since 2016. While the city at large is still working its way back to pre-pandemic standards, asking rent along high-demand corridors like Midtown’s Park Avenue have 2020 in the rearview mirror. Average rents along the avenue jumped 4.6 percent over the quarter, to $119.62 per square foot, in a nearly 14 percent increase from March 2020. Manhattan’s Class A office market commanded $92.19 per square foot last quarter, according to Savills’ second-quarter report, with an overall availability rate
bullets: Manhattan office leasing H1 2026: 23M SF, strongest first-half velocity since 2002; Q2 alone: 11.02M SF.
Positive net absorption 3.51M SF; Simpson Thacher & Bartlett 916K SF lease at 570 Fifth Ave; law firm and tech tenants dominant.
Average asking rents sharp increases; demand outpaced tightening supply.
stories.summary: Manhattan office leasing in H1 2026 totaled nearly 23 million square feet, marking the strongest first-half period since 2002. Q2 delivered 11.02 million square feet of leasing with 3.51 million SF of positive net absorption. Law firms (led by Simpson Thacher & Bartlett's 916K SF lease) and tech companies drove demand; average asking rents increased sharply as supply tightened.
stories.impact: Manhattan office recovery on this scale reduces near-term conversion urgency and improves landlord pricing power, likely extending hold periods for office-to-residential conversion projects. However, this velocity is heavily weighted toward law firms and tech firms seeking trophy space (570 Fifth Ave, etc.), not broad-based demand—developers should stress-test conversion underwriting for sustained office softness if this momentum reverses post-2026 and monitor whether net absorption turns negative again.

stories.title: Miami’s Office Leasing Jumps Nearly 45% in Q2
stories.excerpt: Miami-Dade County’s office market remains strong as companies compete for premium office space, pushing rents to record highs as vacancy continues to tighten. Availability fell to 14.9 percent, down from 17.1 percent a year ago, during the second quarter of 2026, according to data from Savills. Leasing activity jumped to 1.3 million square feet, increasing by nearly 45 percent year-over-year. The average asking rent climbed to $67.12 per square foot, up 7 percent from a year earlier and nearly 57 percent higher than five years ago. The Airport West, Downtown Miami and Coral Gables submarkets accounted for the largest share of leasing activity. Some large transactions included cafe chain Pura Vida relocating its corporate office from Sunset Harbour to the Gateway at Wynwood building, where it leased 26,982 square feet, as well as Planet Fitness taking 26,207 square feet within the Omni Center at 1501 Biscayne Boulevard. Family offices, financial firms, technology companies, law firms — many of them new to the Miami-Dade market — are driving the bulk of the demand at the top end of the market. Miami Beach and Coconut Grove — sought-after residential neighborhoods, where well-paid executives typically live — also boasts the county’s most expensive office rents, which average roughly $99 per square foot. Countywide, rents for Class A buildings rose by 6 percent year-over-year to $80.85 a square foot, reflecting demand for new, amenity-rich properties. Just this month, the family office of billionaire Peter Thiel, a PayPal co-founder and early Facebook investor, signed an 18,158-square-foot lease at Miami’s 830 Brickell, paying $250 per square foot, according to Bloomberg. The price is Miami-Dade County’s most expensive office lease, rivaling rents commanded by some of Manhattan’s premier office towers. Developers of new, luxury buildings are taking note. The office space at the Well, Terra’s mixed-use development in Bay Harbor Islands, is being markete
bullets: Miami-Dade office leasing jumped 45% YoY to 1.3M SF in Q2 2026; availability fell to 14.9% (from 17.1% YoY).
Average asking rent $67.12/SF, +7% YoY, +57% vs. 5-yr avg; Airport West, Downtown Miami, Coral Gables lead activity.
Pura Vida, Planet Fitness relocations; family offices, fintech, law firms, new to market driving demand.
stories.summary: Miami-Dade office leasing surged 45% year-over-year to 1.3 million square feet in Q2 2026, with availability tightening to 14.9% from 17.1% a year prior. Average asking rent climbed to $67.12/SF (+7% YoY, +57% over five years). Airport West, Downtown Miami, and Coral Gables saw largest activity; family offices, fintech, law firms, and corporate relocations drove new demand.
stories.impact: Miami office strength and rising rents signal robust business migration and investor confidence in the market—this typically precedes demand for worker housing and suggests multifamily rents are already pricing in employer concentration. However, Miami's recent rent growth is outpacing income growth; developers should model migration sustainability and monitor whether office momentum pulls forward multifamily demand or stretches affordability limits, which could trigger political/zoning resistance to new supply.

stories.title: Victor Sigoura on how to be a NYC condo developer
stories.excerpt: Victor Sigoura’s first gig at a development firm was working on one of the city’s most iconic and controversial projects.  Sigoura left his job as a lawyer at a white shoe firm to work for El-Ad Group, which had just acquired The Plaza and planned to convert a portion of the hotel into luxury condos. The hotel union fought the conversion, leading to a dramatic escalation. “I had the pleasure of handling the union. I used to get beat up by them every single day, like yelling matches,” said Sigoura, who said El-Ad’s founder Isaac Tshuva and then CEO Miki […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Victor Sigoura, founder Legion Investment Group, building 30-story West Village condo, 20-story Gramercy Park, 26-unit Upper East Side projects.
Legion's first project (109 E. 79th St., 32 units) sold out before TCO at $4K+/SF; 1122 Madison penthouse under contract for $89.5M.
Sigoura transitioned from El-Ad/Naftali Group; focused on high-end condo development in prime NYC neighborhoods.
stories.summary: Victor Sigoura's Legion Investment Group is advancing three large NYC luxury condo projects: a 30-story West Village tower, a 20-story Gramercy Park development, and a 26-unit Upper East Side project. Legion's debut (32 units at 109 E. 79th) nearly sold out pre-TCO at $4K+/SF; a penthouse at 1122 Madison recently went under contract for $89.5M. Sigoura has built Legion into a focused luxury-condo platform after stints at El-Ad and Naftali Group.
stories.impact: NYC luxury condo market remains active at the high end ($4K+/SF, $89M+ penthouses), signaling continued wealth concentration and international capital inflows. However, this is a thin, trophy segment; developers should not extrapolate pricing or absorption to broader multifamily. Bulk-unit rentals face different demand drivers (affordability, job growth, amenities) than condos; monitor whether luxury condo velocity eventually bleeds into rental conversions or if it remains insulated from broader market dynamics.

stories.title: Texas developers thought a state law to expand as-of-right housing production in commercial zones would help them build
stories.excerpt: The Texas legislature’s new law seemed a pro-housing dream. Passed last year, it requires larger municipalities to allow mixed-use and multifamily development in zones that permit office, commercial, retail or warehouse uses. Yet some developers say the law, Senate Bill 840, meant to emancipate builders from restrictions, can’t streamline pre-development negotiations with city officials — some of whom consider the law an encroachment on local authority. “Just because something can be done legally doesn’t mean that things can’t be stalled in the background with all the bureaucracy,” Tanya Ragan, founder of adaptive reuse development company Wildcat Management, said. It’s less […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Texas Senate Bill 840 requires larger cities to allow multifamily/mixed-use in commercial zones but local governments blocking via ordinances.
Arlington, Plano, Irving, Frisco passed height mandates, expensive amenities, industrial rezoning to circumvent state law (effective Sept 1, 2025).
Developers face labyrinthine approvals despite legal-as-of-right status; politicking and persuasion still required.
stories.summary: Texas Senate Bill 840, passed in 2024 and effective September 1, 2025, mandates that larger municipalities allow multifamily and mixed-use development in office, commercial, retail, and warehouse zones. However, North Texas cities including Arlington, Plano, Irving, and Frisco have passed ordinances imposing height restrictions, expensive amenities, and industrial rezoning to circumvent the state law. Developers report that despite SB 840's legal protections, local bureaucracy and political opposition still stall projects.
stories.impact: SB 840 represents a significant pro-housing signal at the state level, but municipal resistance reveals the political friction in high-growth Texas suburbs. Developers should view these anti-housing local ordinances as medium-term regulatory headwinds; expect prolonged entitlement timelines and higher soft costs in Plano, Arlington, Irving, Frisco. Conversely, municipalities that embrace SB 840 (e.g., Austin, San Antonio) may see accelerated approvals and lower landing costs—prioritize friendly jurisdictions and expect value-add opportunities in less-resistant markets.

stories.title: The Closing: Brian Steinwurtzel
stories.excerpt: Brian Steinwurtzel’s grandfather built a real estate dynasty turning old West Side warehouses into office buildings.  Some seven decades later, Steinwurtzel is following the family tradition by converting outdated office buildings into apartments. The 49-year-old has been heading up GFP Real Estate’s developments for the past 13 years. In April, the Gural family spun out the resi-conversion side of the business into GFP Development, naming Steinwurtzel the new entity’s CEO. In recent years, he’s run projects like SoMA — the conversion of the Brutalist-styled 25 Water Street office building into 1,300 rental apartments. He’s also turning the 31-story office tower […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Brian Steinwurtzel, CEO GFP Development (Gural family spin-out), converting office to 1,300 units (SoMA, 25 Water St); 788 units (222 Broadway).
NYC selected GFP to convert 100 Gold Street (city-owned) to 3,700 apartments; grandfather Aaron Gural built dynasty from 1950s warehouse conversions.
Generational adaptive-reuse business; family includes Newmark founder, Flatiron Building conversion underway.
stories.summary: Brian Steinwurtzel, newly named CEO of GFP Development (spun from Gural family's GFP Real Estate), is executing large-scale office-to-multifamily conversions: SoMA (1,300 units at 25 Water Street), 222 Broadway (788 units), and the city-selected 100 Gold Street (3,700 apartments). His grandfather Aaron Gural pioneered warehouse-to-office conversions in the 1950s; the family also owns the Flatiron Building, which is being converted to condos. The business model is generational adaptive reuse.
stories.impact: GFP Development's scale and city partnership on 100 Gold Street (3,700 units) signals that institutional capital is fully committed to large Manhattan office conversions. This validates the long-term supply-and-demand arbitrage (office to rental apartments). However, GFP's success is partly tied to trophy sites (Flatiron, downtown waterfront) and favorable NYC tax incentives; developers should benchmark conversions in secondary/tertiary markets against GFP's metrics while acknowledging potential execution cost and timeline advantages in Manhattan's institutional ecosystem.

stories.title: Hamptons developer Jeremy Morton’s unfinished projects — and debts — stack up
stories.excerpt: You wouldn’t be surprised to meet Jeremy Morton in the Hamptons. Tall and ruddy, Morton rented an apartment in the West Village and owned a 12,800-square-foot home in East Hampton with an infinity pool, pool house and at-home gym on 2 acres.  He married his wife, Brooke, at a black-tie wedding at the Metropolitan Club. She would post about the couple’s lifestyle to her travel blog and Instagram account, both called the Glowtrotter, with pictures detailing their dozens of trips to places like Burning Man, Greece and St. Tropez.  Undergirding the conspicuous consumption was a growing Hamptons real estate portfolio. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Hamptons developer Jeremy Morton has accumulated unfinished projects and debt; $30M Sag Harbor purchase (22K SF) in 2025 (highest retail price ever paid in Hamptons).
Morton owned East Hampton home, West Village apt, Ruschmeyer's nightclub, Cyril's Fish House, Rick's Crabby Cowboy Cafe, retail properties.
Portfolio leveraged via partner capital; conspicuous consumption lifestyle masking overleveraged position.
stories.summary: Hamptons developer Jeremy Morton has taken on multiple unfinished projects while accumulating debt across hospitality, retail, and residential ventures. He paid a record $30M for a 22,000-square-foot Sag Harbor retail corner in 2025. Morton's portfolio includes Ruschmeyer's nightclub, Cyril's Fish House, Rick's Crabby Cowboy Cafe, retail in Water Mill and Southampton, and a custom East Hampton home. Partners report he leveraged assets heavily across properties.
stories.impact: This is a cautionary tale of overleveraging and project concentration in a volatile luxury market (Hamptons). Morton's distress (article appears to track debt accumulation and stalled projects) may lead to forced sales of Hamptons real estate at discounts. Developers in the Hamptons should monitor for forced liquidations and consider opportunistic acquisitions; however, the Hamptons market is micro-cap and seasonal—relevance to multifamily development across broader U.S. markets is negligible.
